title: Chapter 1: The Sealed Mountain
body:
Yan Bo had climbed the Azure Mountain six times since his name was added to the disciple roster, and each time the wind on the eastern ridge had carried a sound he could not place. It was not a voice and not a song, but something in between, as if a stone were trying to remember what it had been before it was a stone.

The elders dismissed the sound as wind moving through a crack in the cliff. Senior Brother Ru, who had walked the ridge for thirty years, said the same. Only the old groundskeeper, who had no rank and no surname, looked at Yan Bo strangely when he asked.

"Boy," the groundskeeper had said, brushing chestnut leaves from the path, "if you hear it, do not turn toward it. And if you turn toward it, do not answer. There are things on this mountain that have been waiting longer than the mountain itself."

That evening, Yan Bo turned toward it. He answered. And somewhere, very far below the ridge, something turned to face him in return.

title: Chapter 2: The Voice Beneath the Path
body:
The cave entrance was no wider than his shoulders, hidden behind a stand of mountain pine that should not have grown at this altitude. Yan Bo squeezed through and the sound, which had been faint on the ridge, suddenly became a single low note that he could feel in his teeth.

The corridor sloped downward for a hundred paces, then opened into a circular chamber lit by nothing he could see. At its center stood a sword driven point-first into the stone floor, its hilt wrapped in faded silk, its blade pitted with something that looked like rust but was not.

When he stepped toward it, the note stopped. The silence that followed was the loudest thing he had ever heard.

"You are late," said a voice from the blade, dry as paper. "Three hundred years late. Sit down. We have a great deal to discuss."

title: Chapter 3: A Borrowed Name
body:
The spirit in the sword did not give Yan Bo its name. It gave him a name to use, which it said was close enough: Qing-Yi, meaning Azure-One. Yan Bo did not believe this was its real name, but the spirit's tone did not invite negotiation.

"You will not tell your sect about me," Qing-Yi said. "You will not bring others here. You will come at the new moon and the full moon, and on those nights I will teach you what no living master will teach you. In exchange, you will eventually return what was taken from me."

Yan Bo, who had not yet agreed to anything, found himself nodding. He noticed that his hand was already resting on the hilt. He did not remember reaching for it.

"Good," said Qing-Yi. "We begin with breathing. Most disciples breathe like dying men. You will breathe like a mountain."

title: Chapter 4: The Mountain's Lung
body:
Breathing like a mountain, it turned out, meant not breathing for very long stretches. Yan Bo passed out four times in his first week. On the fifth attempt he held the breath until the chamber's lamp seemed to dim, and when he exhaled, frost formed on the silk wrapped around the hilt.

"Better," Qing-Yi said. "Now the difficult part. Forget that you breathed at all."

The sect bells rang above ground, calling the disciples to evening rice. Yan Bo did not move. He had only been gone an hour, and he had already begun to suspect that hours on the mountain and hours in the chamber were not the same kind of hours.

title: Chapter 5: Senior Brother Ru's Suspicion
body:
By the end of the second month, Yan Bo had stopped sleeping during the new moon and the full moon, and someone had noticed. Senior Brother Ru cornered him at the wash basins and looked at him for a long time without speaking.

"You have lost weight," Ru said finally. "And gained something else. Your qi is no longer the shape it was. It is the wrong shape for a disciple of your year. Where have you been?"

Yan Bo had practiced the lie for weeks. "The eastern ridge," he said. "For meditation."

Ru's eyes did not move. "The eastern ridge," he repeated, in a voice that meant he did not believe a single syllable of it. "Then you will not mind if I join you tomorrow at moonrise."

title: Chapter 6: Two Sets of Footprints
body:
Yan Bo did not go to the eastern ridge that night, and he did not go to the chamber. He sat in his cell and listened to the bell ring the watches and tried to think of a lie larger than the truth. By dawn he had not slept and he had not invented one.

Qing-Yi, when he finally returned at the next full moon, was not surprised. "Your brother is not a fool," the spirit said. "He will follow you. Let him."

"He will report me to the elders."

"He will report what he sees. What he sees depends on what I allow him to see. Walk in front of me, Yan Bo. Walk slowly. Make no sound."

They climbed. Behind them, two sets of footprints crossed the frost on the path. Only one set continued past the pines.

title: Chapter 7: A Letter from the Valley
body:
A courier from the valley arrived at the sect gates with a sealed letter for Yan Bo. He had not received a letter in three years. The seal was his mother's, and the letter was short.

Your father is dying, it said. Come if you can. Bring nothing. Tell no one which way you came.

Yan Bo brought the letter to the chamber. Qing-Yi read it without being asked, in the way the spirit had of reading things without eyes.

"You will go," said Qing-Yi. "You will take the sword. You will not draw it. There are people on the valley road who have been waiting longer than your father has been ill."

title: Chapter 8: The Valley Road
body:
The valley road in autumn was a brown ribbon between rice paddies that had already been cut. Yan Bo walked it with the sword wrapped in cloth and slung across his back, and he counted the people he passed. Twelve travelers in the first ten li. Three in the second ten. None in the third.

By evening the road was empty in a way that roads are not naturally empty. The crickets had stopped. The wind had stopped. Even the dust under his sandals did not lift.

"Behind you," Qing-Yi said softly. "Five of them. They have been with you since the river crossing."

Yan Bo did not turn. He kept walking, and he began, quietly, to breathe like a mountain.

title: Chapter 9: The Drawn Blade
body:
The ambush happened at a stand of bamboo, where the road bent and the light failed. Five men in unmarked grey stepped out of the bamboo and did not bother to speak. Yan Bo had been told never to draw the sword. He drew the sword.

What happened next he would not be able to describe later. He remembered cold. He remembered a sound like a bell rung underwater. He remembered the bamboo bending the wrong way, as if a wind were pushing it from inside.

When he came back to himself, all five men were on the ground. None of them were dead. None of them were going to stand up again that day either. The sword in his hand was warmer than his hand.

"I told you not to draw it," Qing-Yi said, with something that might have been amusement. "You may now consider yourself someone who does not always listen."

title: Chapter 10: His Father's House
body:
Yan Bo's father did not die that night. He did not die the next night either. He looked at his son for a long time when Yan Bo came through the door, and he said, "You are carrying something you should not be carrying."

Yan Bo did not answer. His mother brought tea. His sisters did not meet his eyes. The sword, leaning in the corner of the room, made no sound, and yet the room was full of the sound it was not making.

At midnight, his father called him to the bedside and said, in a voice barely above a whisper, "Three hundred years ago, our house owed a debt to the Azure Mountain. I had hoped it would pass over you. It has not. Listen carefully. There is a great deal you do not yet know about what you are carrying, and very little time to learn it."

title: Chapter 1: A Tide That Should Not Be There
body:
Mara Penn was twenty-three the first time she noticed that the harbor of Hollow Bay was wrong. The maps in the city archive showed a long curve of stone breakwater enclosing the inner pool. The breakwater, when she walked along it that morning, was forty paces shorter than the chart said it should be. She walked it twice to be sure.

When she returned to the cartographer's loft and reported the discrepancy, Helena Ott did not look up from her ink.

"It will be longer next week," Helena said. "And shorter again the week after. Do not draw it. Do not measure it. Drink your tea."

Mara drank her tea. She had been Helena's apprentice for six years. There were many questions she had stopped asking, and many questions Helena had stopped expecting her to ask. The breakwater was a new one.

title: Chapter 2: The Drowned Cartographer
body:
Helena Ott was found at her desk on a Tuesday in October. Her face was in her ledger. The ledger was wet. The window had been closed all night and the room was on the third floor, far above any tide.

The city watch came and asked questions. Mara answered the ones she could. She did not mention the breakwater. She did not mention the seven other discrepancies she had quietly compiled in a private notebook over the course of three years. She did not mention that, when she had touched Helena's ledger, salt water had come away on her fingers and tasted of a sea she had never been to.

The watch ruled it a stroke. They left. Mara locked the loft, sat down in Helena's chair, and opened the ledger to the last page Helena had written. It was a map. Mara had never seen the coast it depicted, but she recognized the handwriting as her own.

title: Chapter 3: The Inheritance
body:
Helena's will, when the city solicitor finally read it aloud, contained one bequest: every map in the loft, every instrument on the table, and the loft itself, to her apprentice, Mara Penn, on the condition that Mara never sell them and never lend them, and that she continue Helena's work.

Mara had not known there was a work to continue. She had thought there was simply a trade.

The solicitor handed her a sealed envelope marked Open only when you are alone. Then he took his hat and his pity and he left.

Mara waited until dusk. She bolted the loft door. She sat in Helena's chair and she opened the envelope. Inside was a single sentence, written in Helena's careful hand. The coast is moving, the sentence said. We are the only ones who know.

title: Chapter 4: The Notebook Behind the Wall
body:
It took Mara a week to find the second notebook. It was not hidden cleverly. It was hidden honestly, behind a panel in the loft wall that any apprentice would eventually find while sweeping, and any city watchman would never think to look behind.

The notebook was older than Mara. The first entries were not in Helena's hand. The handwriting changed every twenty or thirty pages, but the work continued without interruption: measurements of the harbor, the cliffs, the river mouth, the lighthouse rock. Decade by decade, the same places, drifting.

The last hand in the notebook before Helena's was a woman named Edda Penn, dated forty years before Mara was born. Mara closed the notebook very slowly, set it on the desk, and went downstairs to ask her mother who Edda Penn had been.

title: Chapter 5: A Question for Her Mother
body:
Her mother did not look up from the kettle. "Your great-aunt," she said. "On your father's side. Drowned, I'm told. Long before you were born. We don't speak of her."

"Drowned where?"

"In the bay. In a closed room. Which is why we don't speak of it."

Mara stood in the doorway for a long moment, and then she did something she had not done since she was a child: she sat down at her mother's table without being invited.

"I think," she said, "that I need you to tell me everything you have ever heard about Edda Penn. Even the parts you decided I should not be told."

title: Chapter 6: The Wharfmaster's Lie
body:
The wharfmaster of Hollow Bay was named Idris Foll, and he had held the post for thirty-two years. He greeted Mara in his office with the politeness of a man who had been expecting a less convenient visitor.

"The breakwater," Mara said, without preamble, "is forty paces shorter than the chart."

Foll laughed. The laugh was excellent. It was the laugh of a man who has practiced laughs.

"My dear," he said, "charts are not the coast. The coast is not the chart. You have inherited a difficult occupation. Take some time. Walk in the hills. Stop measuring the breakwater."

When Mara left his office, she noticed that the inkwell on his desk had been moved very slightly to cover a stain on the ledger underneath. The stain was salt water. She had touched it on the way in. It had not been there an hour earlier.

title: Chapter 7: A Customer for a Closed Shop
body:
Mara had hung the closed sign on the loft door for a month, and yet on the twenty-eighth day someone climbed the stairs and knocked. A man in a dark coat that smelled faintly of pine. He did not give his name. He wanted, he said, a map.

"The loft is closed," Mara said.

"Not to me," said the man, and he set a copper coin on the table. The coin was not local. The coin was, in fact, a kind of coin that had not been minted in Hollow Bay for two hundred years.

"What map?" Mara said.

"The one you have not finished," said the man. "The one in your great-aunt's notebook. You have three weeks. After that, others will come who are less patient than I am, and the price will not be a coin."

title: Chapter 8: A Night on the Breakwater
body:
Mara walked the breakwater at low tide that night, with a hooded lantern and a measuring chain. The chain reached the seaward end forty paces sooner than it should have. The water beyond the end was not the water she expected. It was clear in a way that the bay had never been clear. She could see straight to the bottom, and the bottom was not stone.

It was a road. A paved road, running out into deep water, leading somewhere.

She lifted the lantern. The road ran out beyond the lamplight in a perfectly straight line.

For a long minute Mara stood at the very end of the breakwater and considered taking a single step forward onto the road. She did not. She turned around. She walked back. She wrote nothing in the loft notebook that night, because some things, she was beginning to understand, must be remembered without being recorded.

title: Chapter 9: Helena's Apprentice Before Mara
body:
There had been another apprentice before Mara. Mara had always known this. Helena had never spoken of him. His name was Jorin, and he had left the loft eleven years ago without explanation, and Helena had taken Mara on the next spring.

Mara found Jorin in a tavern on the lower docks, exactly where her mother had hinted he might be. He had not aged well. He looked at her over a chipped cup of beer and his face did three things in quick succession: recognition, fear, and resignation.

"You walked the breakwater," he said. It was not a question.

"Yes."

"And you saw the road."

"Yes."

He drank, slowly, and set the cup down. "Then you are already past the point where I can help you. I am sorry, girl. I am very sorry. But sit down anyway. There are at least a few things I can tell you that may keep you alive until midwinter."

title: Chapter 10: The First Map of the Other Coast
body:
On the night of the winter solstice, Mara sat at Helena's desk and began to draw. She drew without measurements and without instruments. The pen moved as if it knew the coastline already, as if it had drawn this coast many times before in many other hands.

When she stopped, three hours later, she had drawn a coast that did not match Hollow Bay and did not match anywhere on any chart she had ever seen. It had a harbor, and a breakwater, and a lighthouse on a rock. The breakwater was forty paces longer than the one in Hollow Bay.

She pinned the map to the wall. She poured herself a small cup of Helena's wine. She raised the cup to the empty chair across the desk.

"Helena," she said quietly, "I see what you were doing. I will continue. But I will continue differently. And before this is done, I will know whose coast this is."

title: Chapter 1: Cultivar 947
body:
The Bureau of Cultivars licensed every plant in the city of Korr. Each citizen kept a registry of their household greenery, and each registered cultivar carried a serial number that began with the year of patent and ended with the licensee's signature.

There were no unregistered plants. There had not been for sixty years. The penalty for cultivating one was loss of housing, loss of employment, and one year of remedial agricultural service in the outer fields.

Mira Kade, junior botanist at Greenhouse District Seven, had handled forty-eight thousand seeds in her career. She had never seen a seed she did not recognize. Until Tuesday.

title: Chapter 2: A Pocket of Soil
body:
The seed was the size of a peppercorn and the color of nothing she had a word for. It had appeared in the bottom of a soil shipment from the western depot, half buried, half visible.

Procedure was clear: bag the seed, log the serial number on the shipment manifest, and submit a Form 7 to the Bureau within four working hours. Mira did the first two and not the third. She did not, when she went home that evening, throw the bag away. She did not, the next morning, mention it to her supervisor.

She put the seed in a small pot of unregistered loam that she had been keeping in a kitchen drawer for reasons she had never thought to examine. She watered it once. She closed the drawer.

title: Chapter 3: The Drawer Will Not Close
body:
On the fourth day, the drawer would not close. Mira pulled the pot out and stared at it. The seed had grown a single pale shoot, eight centimeters long, curled tightly in a spiral. She had not watered it again. There had been no light in the drawer.

She moved the pot to the windowsill and locked her apartment door, then unlocked it, then locked it again. She made tea and forgot to drink it.

By the time the tea was cold the shoot had unfurled three small leaves. The leaves were the same color as the seed had been. Mira found, on close inspection, that she could not focus her eyes on the color directly; it slipped sideways from her attention every time she tried.

title: Chapter 4: An Inspector Arrives
body:
On the eighth day, Bureau Inspector Vell rang the bell of Apartment 12B. Mira had ten seconds to decide what to do with a plant that had now reached the height of her wrist. She lifted the pot, opened the cabinet under the sink, set the pot inside, closed the cabinet, and opened the door.

Inspector Vell was small, gray, and pleasant. He apologized for the visit. He explained that a routine soil audit had flagged a missing manifest entry from the western depot. He had a list of recipients. Could he, very briefly, look around?

Mira nodded and stepped aside. Inspector Vell walked once through the apartment. He paused in the kitchen. He did not open the cabinet under the sink. He paused, very slightly, beside the cabinet. Then he thanked her and left.

title: Chapter 5: A Neighbor's Window
body:
Across the courtyard, an old man named Tier Holm watched Inspector Vell leave Mira's building. Tier had lived in Korr longer than Mira had been alive. He had once been a botanist himself, before he had refused to sign a manifest he disagreed with and lost his housing.

That night Tier wrote Mira a note. He slid it under her door at three in the morning, without knocking. The note said only: If it has begun to remember, you must not let them take it. They have been trying to kill the last of these for sixty years.

Mira read the note four times. She put it in the kettle and lit the kettle on fire. She watched the paper curl. She went to the cabinet under the sink and looked at the plant. It had grown another leaf. The leaf was a color she could now see directly, for the first time.

title: Chapter 6: A Knock at Three
body:
Tier knocked the next night at exactly the same hour. Mira opened the door without speaking. He came in. He did not look at the cabinet. He sat at the kitchen table and waited.

"Why did you come," Mira said.

"Because Inspector Vell will come back," Tier said. "And the next time he will open the cabinet."

"How do you know."

"Because I was Inspector Vell, for eleven years, before I was the old man at the window. The cabinet under the sink is the third place we are trained to look. Not the first. The third. He did not open it because he wanted you to think he had not noticed. He noticed, girl. He noticed everything."

title: Chapter 7: An Unregistered Garden
body:
Tier led Mira, the next night, down a stairwell she had never used, into a sub-basement that the building registry did not acknowledge existed. The room at the bottom was warm. It smelled of soil and something faintly metallic.

There were eleven plants in the room. None of them had serial numbers. Several of them were the color Mira had been unable to look at directly until two days ago.

"How many of you," she said.

"In this city," Tier said, "forty-three. That we know of. Probably more. We do not introduce ourselves to each other unless we must."

"And you brought me here."

"Yes," Tier said. "Because you have already become one of us. You did the moment you did not file Form 7. The rest is paperwork. The paperwork can be done quickly if you are willing."

title: Chapter 8: Form 7
body:
Mira did not, in the end, file Form 7. She filed a different form, written by Tier on paper that had not come from a city stationer. The form was short. It said only: I have seen what was buried. I will not bury it again.

She signed it. Tier folded it. He took it away.

Three days later, Inspector Vell returned to Apartment 12B. He was less pleasant this time. He opened the cabinet under the sink. The cabinet was empty. He looked at Mira for a long minute.

"You are a very lucky young woman," he said finally. "For now."

Mira nodded. She did not feel lucky. She felt, for the first time in her life, that she was standing on a road that had a direction.

title: Chapter 9: The Bureau's Memory
body:
The Bureau's archive went back two hundred years. The first hundred years were a matter of public record. The second hundred were not. Tier had a key to a section of the second hundred. He had kept the key for eleven years, against the day someone would need it.

That day was now. Mira sat in the archive at four in the morning, by the light of a single lamp, and read.

The city, the record said, had once had many plants. The plants had once had names that were not serial numbers. There had been a year, sixty-one years ago, in which a great many of these plants had simply disappeared from the record, all at once. The reason given in the official summary was a sentence Mira had to read three times to be sure she had understood it.

The plants, the summary said, had asked too many questions.

title: Chapter 10: A Garden That Asks
body:
Mira returned to the sub-basement at dawn. The plants were waiting. She did not know how she knew they were waiting, but she knew.

She sat down on the floor between the rows. She set the seed pot, with its small bright unregistered shoot, in front of her.

"Ask me," she said. "I will try to answer."

The plants did not speak. They did not need to. The question rose in her chest, fully formed, as if it had been waiting in her for sixty-one years.

Why, the question said, did you forget us.

Mira did not answer. She did not have an answer yet. But she put her hand on the rim of the small pot and she promised, without words, that she would learn one.

title: Chapter 1: The Lighthouse at Ilha Branca
body:
The lighthouse on Ilha Branca was old enough that its keeper, Diogo, had known three previous keepers as a boy. The lamp ran on oil that was delivered by boat once a month. The boat sometimes came on time. More often it did not.

Diogo had been the keeper for eleven years. In all that time he had received exactly seven personal letters: four from his sister, two from his mother before she died, and one from a man who turned out to have written to the wrong lighthouse.

On the morning of the eighth letter, the boat brought a single envelope addressed in handwriting Diogo did not recognize, postmarked from a town he had never heard of.

title: Chapter 2: The First Letter
body:
The letter said:

Keeper of Ilha Branca,

I have watched your light from my window every night for two years. I do not know if you exist. I am told my shore does not appear on the charts of your country, and that your shore does not appear on mine. If you receive this, please write back. I would very much like to know what you see at sunset.

— Bel

Diogo read the letter four times. He looked at the envelope. There was no return address that any postal system he knew would recognize, only a single word: Casa.

He folded the letter very carefully. He set it on the kitchen table. He spent the rest of the day going about his work in a state of considerable distraction. At sunset, he sat down at the table and began to write his reply, although he had no idea where he was going to send it.

title: Chapter 3: A Reply with Nowhere to Go
body:
Diogo's reply was three pages long. He described the colors of the sunset in detail he had not thought he was capable of describing. He described the sound the lamp made when it first caught. He described the gulls. He described, very briefly, his sister, his mother, and the man who had once written to the wrong lighthouse.

He did not, in the end, know what to do with the reply. He placed it in an envelope. He wrote Bel on the outside. He set the envelope on top of the unanswered letter, on the kitchen table.

In the morning, both envelopes were gone. In their place was a second letter, in the same handwriting as the first.

Thank you, the second letter began. You write more than I expected. Please write more.

title: Chapter 4: The Habit of the Table
body:
Diogo began to leave letters on the table every evening. They were gone every morning. The replies appeared in their place, sometimes within hours, sometimes after a delay of several days.

He asked her, in his fourth letter, what her town looked like. She replied with a description of a fishing port surrounded by olive trees, where the bell of the church rang at sunset and the gulls were larger than his.

He asked her, in his sixth letter, what country she lived in. She replied that she did not know how to translate her country into his alphabet. The name, she said, was something like Casa, but Casa was also the word for home in her language, and she could not be sure she was not simply confusing the two.

title: Chapter 5: A Pressed Flower
body:
After two months, Bel sent him a pressed flower. It was bright orange and shaped like nothing that grew on Ilha Branca, and it had no smell except a faint smell of salt.

Diogo placed the flower in his oldest book, between the pages where he had been keeping a postcard from his sister. The postcard, when he checked the next day, was gone. The flower was still there. He did not know what this meant.

He wrote to Bel that the flower had arrived safely. He wrote that he wished he had something equally bright to send back. He went out, the next afternoon, and gathered three small white stones from the beach, and set them in an envelope.

The stones were gone in the morning. Bel's reply said: They are beautiful. They are warm. How can stones be warm?

title: Chapter 6: The Boat Misses a Month
body:
The supply boat did not come the following month. This had happened before. Diogo rationed the oil. He cut down on the lamp's evening hour and made up for it by burning it longer through the small hours. He ate the last of the dried fish and started on the older biscuits.

He did not write to Bel about any of this. He did not want her to worry. He wrote, instead, about the way the moon was unusually low on the horizon this week, and how the gulls had begun to gather in larger numbers than he could remember from any previous spring.

Bel's reply, when it came, was shorter than usual. The boat will come tomorrow, she wrote. I cannot tell you how I know. I am sorry that I know.

title: Chapter 7: The Boat Comes
body:
The boat came in the morning, as Bel had said. The captain apologized for the delay. He blamed weather. He unloaded the oil and the biscuits and a fresh sack of beans, and he stayed long enough for a cup of tea, and then he left.

Diogo, that night, sat at the table for a long time before writing his reply. Eventually he wrote: I do not understand how you knew about the boat. I do not understand many things about you. I am sixty-three years old. I have not been good at letters for most of my life. But I will keep writing as long as you keep replying, because the alternative now seems unimaginable.

Bel's reply was a single line. So will I, it said. So will I.

title: Chapter 8: A Visitor From the Mainland
body:
A young inspector came from the mainland that autumn, on a routine survey of the lighthouse and its accounts. He stayed three nights. He noted the unusually low oil consumption. He noted the careful state of the lamp. He noted, on the third evening, the envelopes stacked very neatly on the corner of the kitchen table.

"Family?" he asked politely.

"A correspondent," Diogo said.

The inspector smiled and let the matter rest. He was, Diogo realized afterward, the first person in eleven years to whom he had mentioned Bel out loud. Diogo did not know how he felt about this. He decided, after some thought, that he felt fine. He decided that some small part of Bel's existence was now witnessed by a third person, and that this was probably a good thing.

title: Chapter 9: A Winter Without Letters
body:
In the winter of his thirteenth year as keeper, the letters stopped. Diogo wrote and wrote, and his letters disappeared from the table in the morning, but no replies came in their place.

For three weeks he tried to convince himself that this was a delay like the supply boat's delay. By the fourth week, he stopped sleeping. By the sixth week, he wrote a letter that began, Bel, I am afraid for you, and ended, If you cannot reply, leave a stone on the table. Any stone. So that I know you can still hear me.

The next morning, there was a small white stone on the table. It was one of the stones Diogo himself had sent her in the first year of letters. It was warm to the touch.

title: Chapter 10: The Light That Did Not Belong to Either Coast
body:
On the night of the equinox, Diogo saw a second light on the horizon. It was not the light of any ship he knew. It was steady, and it was on a part of the horizon where no land was supposed to be, and it answered the rhythm of his lamp by a count of three.

He answered, again, by a count of five.

The second light answered by a count of seven.

This continued for most of the night. By morning the second light was gone, and a letter from Bel was on the table. The letter said only: I saw you. I have been seeing you for thirteen years. Last night was the first night you saw me. I do not know what this means. I will keep writing. So will you.

title: Chapter 1: The Grief Engine
body:
Mira Almeida was thirty-four years old, and she had spent eight of those years building recommendation engines for grief. The company she co-founded had a quietly clinical name, Resonance, and a tagline that her co-founder had written and that Mira had never been able to fully approve of: We help you remember in the way that helps.

The product worked. The customers, when surveyed, reported lower rates of intrusive memory and higher rates of what the team called "chosen recall." The reviews were good. The board was happy. Mira was, on most days, also happy.

On the morning of the day a new client booked a call, Mira was happy. By the end of that call, she was not.

title: Chapter 2: The Request
body:
The client was a man named Henrik. He was the brother of a recently deceased pianist. He had paid for the highest tier of the service.

"I have a request," Henrik said. "I would like the system to recommend a way to forget him."

Mira said nothing for a long moment. The product did not do this. The product was, in fact, explicitly designed not to do this. It was a recommendation engine for selective remembrance. The selectivity went one way.

"That is not what we do," she said carefully.

"I know," said Henrik. "But I would like to ask the system anyway. I have read the technical documentation. The model is capable. The product policy is a separate decision. I would like to ask the model."

title: Chapter 3: The Inside of the Policy
body:
Mira spent the rest of the day with the policy document. The document was sixty pages long. Mira had written eight of those pages herself, four years ago, on a flight from Lisbon to San Francisco.

The relevant section was on page forty-two. It said that the product would never recommend an action whose intent was to erase a relationship. It said this in three slightly different ways. None of the three was a technical limit. All of them were policy.

Mira read the section four times. Then she wrote a memo to herself. The memo said: We told the model not to. We did not tell the model that it could not. We assumed those were the same thing. They are not the same thing.

title: Chapter 4: The Co-founder's Couch
body:
Mira's co-founder, Yuna, lived in a one-bedroom apartment on a hill overlooking a freeway. The couch in the living room was the same couch they had used in the first month of the company, when neither of them could afford an office. Mira had spent more nights on that couch than she had spent in any other room outside her own home.

She took her memo to Yuna. Yuna read it. Yuna sat with it for a long time before speaking.

"We need to decide," Yuna said finally, "whether the policy is the product or the policy is the wrapper around the product. I have been assuming the first thing. After reading this, I am not sure anymore."

"Neither am I," Mira said. "That is the entire problem."

title: Chapter 5: An Honest Email to a Client
body:
Mira wrote Henrik an honest email. The email said that Resonance would not, as a matter of policy, recommend ways to forget. The email said that the model was technically capable of being asked the question, and that Mira had personally decided not to do so. The email said that this decision was Mira's, and that she would explain it on a call if Henrik wished.

Henrik replied within twenty minutes. He thanked her for the honesty. He requested the call.

The call, when it happened, lasted two hours. By the end of it Mira had cried twice and Henrik had cried once and they had agreed on nothing, except that they would speak again in a month.

title: Chapter 6: The Board Meeting
body:
The quarterly board meeting was on a Tuesday. Mira walked into it knowing what she was going to say. She said it in the third agenda item. She said that the company was going to publish, by the end of the quarter, a public account of what the system could not be asked to do, and a public reasoning for those limits.

The board, when Mira finished, was silent in the careful way that boards are silent when they have not yet decided whether to be angry.

The chair of the board, a quiet man named Frederik, said only, "We will discuss the scope of the publication. We will not discuss whether to publish. Mira is right that we publish. Mira is right that we explain. The question is only how much to explain. We will spend the next forty minutes on that, and not on whether."

title: Chapter 7: The Document
body:
The document was eleven pages long when Mira finished the first draft. It was nine pages long when Yuna finished editing it. It was seven pages long when legal finished editing it. It was, in the end, four pages long.

Mira had expected to be furious about the cuts. She was not furious. The four pages that remained were, somehow, more honest than the eleven pages had been. The cuts had not removed the truth. They had removed her need to be praised for saying it.

She signed the document on a Thursday. Yuna signed it on the same Thursday. The company published it the following Monday at nine in the morning. By noon, the story was the second most-read article on three different technology publications. By evening, it was the first.

title: Chapter 8: A Second Call With Henrik
body:
Henrik called Mira the day the document was published. He did not request a forgetting protocol. He requested, instead, the highest tier of the standard remembrance product.

"I read what you published," he said. "I think you are wrong about one or two things. I think you are right about most of it. I would like to use the product you actually make. I would like, eventually, to disagree with you about the parts I think are wrong, but not today. Today I would just like to use it."

"Of course," Mira said.

They did not, after the call, speak again for nearly a year. When they did, it was Henrik who called Mira, and not the other way around, and the conversation was an entirely different conversation than either of them had expected.

title: Chapter 9: A Sunday
body:
On the first Sunday of summer, Mira did not work. She walked to the small park near her apartment with a paperback book and she did not open it. She watched two children try and fail to teach a dog to sit. She watched a couple argue gently about which path to take home. She watched an old woman feed pieces of bread to pigeons and to one cautious crow.

She thought about Henrik. She thought about Yuna. She thought about the eleven pages, and the seven pages, and the four pages. She thought about the parts of her job that the four pages did not yet describe.

She took a small notebook out of her pocket and she began to write the next document. It would be longer than the first. It would be honest in a different way. It would, she suspected, take her the rest of her career to finish.

title: Chapter 10: The Recommendation
body:
On the last day of that summer, Mira asked the system a question of her own. She did not ask it to recommend a way to forget. She did not ask it to recommend a way to remember.

She asked it to recommend a way to keep working on the thing she was working on, when she was tired, and when the work was harder than she had thought it would be, and when she suspected that the work would never be finished by her or by anyone else.

The system thought for a long moment. The system was not supposed to think for long moments. Mira waited.

The answer, when it came, was a single sentence. The sentence was not from any of Mira's training corpora. The sentence said only: Keep writing the document. Keep listening to the people who use it. Stop, when you must, but do not stop because you are afraid of being wrong. Mira wrote the sentence down in her notebook. Then she went home.
